pattern: Interval dynamic programming over prefix sums computes the cheapest way to coalesce each sub-row into the fewest possible piles, only charging the merge cost when a span finally collapses to one pile.
hints: Merging is only possible at all when the number of piles minus one is divisible by k minus one.
Think about the best cost to reduce a range into one pile, built from the best costs of splitting it into compatible smaller groups.

Let dp over an interval be the minimum cost to leave it as few piles as possible; combine subintervals whose lengths line up modulo k minus one, and when a span's length permits a single pile add the range's total stone sum once.

edge_inputs.description: impossible because the count never reduces to one
single pile is already merged at zero cost
two piles cannot be merged when k exceeds the count
exactly k piles merge in one step
all equal piles with pairwise merges

stress.description: random small row with a feasible k chosen to allow full merge when possible
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 24))
    stones = [rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(2, max(2, min(n, 6)))
    return [stones, k]
stress.note: small n keeps the cubic-plus interval DP fast; some cases are infeasible (-1)
